---
title: "Distance Does Not Explain Price"
subtitle: "How airline market power shapes what you pay"
author: "Akshay Arun, Alexa Nakanishi, & Norman Wang"
format:
  html:
    theme: yeti
    toc: true
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5
bibliography: ../assets/references.bib
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

Have you ever felt like airline ticket prices have surged after you start researching flights? Have you ever been confused by the price of flights given the distance you are traveling?


![Deleted JetBlue Tweet. Source: https://x.com/CultureCrave/status/2046631236941295920](images/jetblue-tweet.png)

In a now deleted tweet, JetBlue has, it seems, admitted to surveillance pricing. Surveillance pricing, also known as “personalized algorithmic pricing”, is a tactic used by companies where prices change dynamically based on the data of individual consumers [@barton_surveillance_pricing]. While federal-level investigations by the Federal Trade Commission into surveillance pricing have approached a standstill, many states have begun cracking down on it. In fact, Maryland is on track to become the first state to ban surveillance pricing for groceries [@npr_surveillance_pricing_maryland]. Yet, surveillance pricing may be true and real for airlines.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

![Deleted Surveillance Pricing Tweet. Source: https://www.instagram.com/p/DXmmV6GDbMx/](images/surveillance.png){width="100%"} 

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

![Airline Pricing Thread. Source: https://www.instagram.com/p/DXKSSiojjhl/](images/kalesalad-thread.png){width="100%"}

:::

::::

Since the deleted tweet, a lawsuit has been filed against JetBlue for collecting consumer data without consent and using such data to set ticket prices, and others have taken to social media to express their thoughts and frustrations with the airline system in the United States [@cbs_jetblue_surveillance_pricing].


Buying plane tickets has always been more complicated than simply buying your groceries. Even before this scandal, there still existed tips and tricks on how to ‘game’ the system to get the cheapest airline tickets possible. Influencers on social media have entire brands that are built around this. 

![](images/instagram.PNG){width="25%" fig-align="center"} 


But, do flight prices truly vary so randomly? What does the data actually say? See the following visualizations below to learn about the relationship between flight fares and prices. 

This data visualization project uses data from the Bureau of Transportation Statistics (BTS) DB1B Market survey, covering US domestic airline routes from Q3 2024 to Q2 2025, with over 25 million ticketing records.


## Flight Fare by Distance

How strongly is your airline ticket price related to the distance of the flight? 

Unsurprisingly, longer flights tend to have higher median flight prices. But perhaps surprisingly, as the length of your flight increases, the median ticket price of the flight does not increase dramatically. Viewing the farthest left boxplot and the farthest right boxplot, the median price of a flight that covers a distance of 100-500 miles is only slightly higher than the median price of a flight that covers the distance of 2300-4200 miles. 

We also see that while the range of these flight fares increases as the distance you fly increases, the minimum ticket price of each distance group is the equal across all distance groups. In other words, a the cheapest ticket price for a flight that is 100-500 miles long and the cheapest ticekt price for a flight that is 2300-4200 miles long could actually be the same.

![](images/static_viz_1.png)

So, does a longer flight necessarily mean a higher ticket price? Upon a first glance, yes, but certainly there seem to be other factors at play. If higher ticket prices were determined solely by flight distance, the difference in flight fares would vary significantly more than they do here.

## Flight Fare by Route

How strongly are your flight fare and the airlines you fly out of and into related?

Looking at the top 10 most popular routes, we see that depending on the route you fly, your airline ticket can vary vastly. For example, if you fly from the San Francisco International Airport (SFO) to the San Diego International Airport (SAN), you could expect the price of your flight to fall within a relatively narrow range. But, if you fly from John F Kennedy International Airport (JFK) in New York to Los Angeles International Airport (LAX), the price of your flight could be as cheap as $250, but also as expensive as almost $2000. 

![](images/static_viz_2.png)



## Visualizing Popular Flight Paths

What are the most popular flight routes in the United States? 

The interactive map below shows the top 200 busiest US domestic routes. Line color indicates average fare, where the darker the blue, the more expensive the fare, and line thickness reflects total passengers, where the thicker the line the more passengers the route has served. Grey dots represent each airport. 

Hover over any route for details on the average fare of the route, the distance of the route, and the total number of passengers that this route has seen.

<iframe
  src="images/interactive_viz_1-200.html"
  width="100%"
  height="600"
  style="border: none;">
</iframe>


## Fewer Carriers, Higher Fares

When fewer airlines compete at an airport, fares tend to be higher. Each dot below represents a US airport, sized by passenger volume. The dashed trendline and correlation coefficient confirm this relationship: less competition means higher prices.

<iframe
  src="images/interactive_viz_2.html"
  width="100%"
  height="520"
  style="border: none;">
</iframe>

## Airport Market Structure: Linked View

The interactive map below shows US airports colored by number of carriers. Click any airport to highlight its position in the scatterplot on the right, which shows HHI (Herfindahl-Hirschman Index), a measure of market concentration, against average fare. Higher HHI means less competition.

<iframe
  src="images/linked_view.html"
  width="100%"
  height="500"
  style="border: none;">
</iframe>

## Infographic

Double marginalization occurs when a manufacturer and a retailer both mark up the price of a product, leading to higher prices for consumers. In the airline industry, this can happen when airlines sell tickets through a third-party flight operator that also take a commission/contact fee. The infographic below illustrates this concept in the context of airline pricing.

![Airline Pricing Infographic](images/infographic.png)

## Flight Price & Route Explorer

The Flight Price & Route Explorer page was created as a tool to enable users to compare flight prices and distance for different flight routes. This application also provides an AI-generated flight summary and route recommendation to help users pick the best route option for their needs.

To view the Flight Price & Route Explorer, click the button below. 

[View Flight Price & Route Explorer](https://5200-data-viz.streamlit.app/){.btn .btn-primary target="_blank"}

# Conclusion

Distance alone does not explain what you pay for a flight. Market structure matters. Airports served by fewer carriers charge systematically higher fares, and our data confirms this across hundreds of US airports. A flight from Boston to DC can cost more than a transatlantic route of similar distance, simply because one market is dominated by a single carrier. As investigations into surveillance pricing continue and states push for new consumer protections, the data is clear: less competition means higher prices, and that cost is paid by everyday travelers.
